title: Locus (genetics)

infobox.value: The position is on the chromosome's short arm (a common apocryphal explanation is that the *p* stands for *petit* in French); *q* indicates the long arm (chosen as next letter in alphabet after p; it is also said that *q* stands for *queue*, meaning "tail" in French[[4]](#cite_note-4)).

The numbers that follow the letter represent the position on the arm: region 2, band 2, sub-band 1. The bands are visible under a [microscope](/wiki/Microscope "Microscope") when the [chromosome](/wiki/Chromosome "Chromosome") is [suitably stained](/wiki/G_banding "G banding"). Each of the bands are numbered, beginning with 1 for the band nearest the [centromere](/wiki/Centromere "Centromere"). Sub-bands and sub-sub-bands are visible at higher resolution.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

(pl.: **loci**) is a specific, fixed position on a [chromosome](/wiki/Chromosome "Chromosome") where a particular [gene](/wiki/Gene "Gene") or [genetic marker](/wiki/Genetic_marker "Genetic marker") is located.[[1]](#cite_note-1) Each chromosome carries many genes, with each gene occupying a different position or locus; in humans, the total number of [protein-coding genes](/wiki/Human_genome#Coding_sequences_(protein-coding_genes) "Human genome") in a complete [haploid](/wiki/Haploid "Haploid") set of 23 chromosomes is estimated at 19,000–20,000.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

or **p-arm**, while the longer arm is the **q arm** or **q-arm**. The chromosomal locus of a typical gene, for example, might be written *3p22.1*, where:[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[doi](/wiki/Doi_(identifier) "Doi (identifier)"):[10.1093/hmg/ddu309](https://doi.org/10.1093%2Fhmg%2Fddu309). From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Location of a gene or region on a chromosome

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0b/Chromosome.svg/250px-Chromosome.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Chromosome.svg)

Parts of a typical chromosome:  
  
(1) [Chromatid](/wiki/Chromatid "Chromatid")  
(2) [Centromere](/wiki/Centromere "Centromere")  
(3) Short (p) arm  
(4) Long (q) arm

In [genetics](/wiki/Genetics "Genetics"), a **locus** (pl.: **loci**) is a specific, fixed position on a [chromosome](/wiki/Chromosome "Chromosome") where a particular [gene](/wiki/Gene "Gene") or [genetic marker](/wiki/Genetic_marker "Genetic marker") is located.[[1]](#cite_note-1) Each chromosome carries many genes, with each gene occupying a different position or locus; in humans, the total number of [protein-coding genes](/wiki/Human_genome#Coding_sequences_(protein-coding_genes) "Human genome") in a complete [haploid](/wiki/Haploid "Haploid") set of 23 chromosomes is estimated at 19,000–20,000.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

Genes may possess multiple variants known as [alleles](/wiki/Allele "Allele"), and an allele may also be said to reside at a particular locus. [Diploid](/wiki/Diploid "Diploid") and [polyploid](/wiki/Polyploid "Polyploid") cells whose chromosomes have the same allele at a given locus are called [homozygous](/wiki/Homozygote "Homozygote") with respect to that locus, while those that have different alleles at a given locus are called [heterozygous](/wiki/Heterozygote "Heterozygote").[[3]](#cite_note-3) The ordered list of loci known for a particular [genome](/wiki/Genome "Genome") is called a [gene map](/wiki/Gene_map "Gene map"). [Gene mapping](/wiki/Gene_mapping "Gene mapping") is the process of determining the specific locus or loci responsible for producing a particular [phenotype](/wiki/Phenotype "Phenotype") or [biological trait](/wiki/Biological_trait "Biological trait"). [Association mapping](/wiki/Association_mapping "Association mapping"), also known as "linkage disequilibrium mapping", is a method of mapping quantitative trait loci (QTLs) that takes advantage of historic linkage disequilibrium to link phenotypes (observable characteristics) to genotypes (the genetic constitution of organisms), uncovering genetic associations.

## Nomenclature

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Locus_(genetics)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Nomenclature")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0f/Cytogenetic_Banding_Nomenclature.png/250px-Cytogenetic_Banding_Nomenclature.png)](/wiki/File:Cytogenetic_Banding_Nomenclature.png)

[Cytogenetic banding](/wiki/Karyotype#Types_of_banding "Karyotype") nomenclature

The shorter arm of a chromosome is termed the **p arm** or **p-arm**, while the longer arm is the **q arm** or **q-arm**. The chromosomal locus of a typical gene, for example, might be written *3p22.1*, where:[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

* *3* = [chromosome 3](/wiki/Chromosome_3_(human) "Chromosome 3 (human)")
* *p* = p-arm
* *22* = region 2, [band](/wiki/Karyotype#Types_of_banding "Karyotype") 2 (read as "two, two", not "twenty-two")
* *1* = sub-band 1

Thus the entire locus of the example above would be read as "three P two two point one". The [cytogenetic bands](/wiki/Chromosome_banding "Chromosome banding") are areas of the chromosome either rich in actively-transcribed DNA ([euchromatin](/wiki/Euchromatin "Euchromatin")) or packaged DNA ([heterochromatin](/wiki/Heterochromatin "Heterochromatin")). They appear differently upon staining (for example, euchromatin appears white and heterochromatin appears black on [Giemsa staining](/wiki/Giemsa_staining "Giemsa staining")). They are counted from the [centromere](/wiki/Centromere "Centromere") out toward the [telomeres](/wiki/Telomere "Telomere").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/11/Neurofibromatosis2-locus.svg/60px-Neurofibromatosis2-locus.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Neurofibromatosis2-locus.svg)

Example of cytogenetic bands

| Component | Explanation |
| --- | --- |
| *3* | The chromosome number |
| *p* | The position is on the chromosome's short arm (a common apocryphal explanation is that the *p* stands for *petit* in French); *q* indicates the long arm (chosen as next letter in alphabet after p; it is also said that *q* stands for *queue*, meaning "tail" in French[[4]](#cite_note-4)). |
| *22.1* | The numbers that follow the letter represent the position on the arm: region 2, band 2, sub-band 1. The bands are visible under a [microscope](/wiki/Microscope "Microscope") when the [chromosome](/wiki/Chromosome "Chromosome") is [suitably stained](/wiki/G_banding "G banding"). Each of the bands are numbered, beginning with 1 for the band nearest the [centromere](/wiki/Centromere "Centromere"). Sub-bands and sub-sub-bands are visible at higher resolution.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] |

A range of loci is specified in a similar way. For example, the locus of gene [OCA1](/wiki/OCA1 "OCA1") may be written "11q1.4-q2.1", meaning it is on the long arm of chromosome 11, somewhere in the range from sub-band 4 of region 1 to sub-band 1 of region 2.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The ends of a chromosome are labeled *"pter"* and *"qter"*, and so *"2qter"* refers to the terminus of the long arm of chromosome 2.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
